We will throw error if users don't have this installed. After that, `mega-sync` will throw errors if users are not logged in... or when things does not go its way. That's when we can deter responsibility to `mega-sync` and not having to make custom errors... And also because we don't know how `mega-sync` work.

To reiterate...
* Not installed? We throw an error.
* Users not signed in? `mega-sync` throw an error. We deter everything here.
* Users signed in + no number of syncs... `mega-sync` does not throw an error.
* Users signed in, but no output? `mega-sync` does not throw an error, but you want to...
* Users does not have Internet, `mega-sync` does not throw an error, but your code might...

We also don't throw errors on other things such as...

* No Internet connection on `whatismyip`.
* Audio players not configured or running (iirc mpd throws error, grrr.)
* No new mails, timew intervals, tasks, updates, etc.
* et cetera

Generally, we want to go for fewer configs, fewer errors, fewer issues, etc.

Users seeing `No MEGA` can figure out their issues fast because they're smart cookies and they already run linux, i3wm, and py3status too... Also because `mega-sync` does not consider this a fatal error in first place so basically, both `mega-sync` and we are saying that users can set this up later when they want to... instead of trying to make an error on possibly something trivial.

1. The problem is that workers that are kept-alive never terminate if any tasks become DISABLED. So although currently luigi won't run a task if its dependencies are DISABLED, it also won't terminate, since it'll never try to run a disabled task, so therefore will never fail, so therefore will never be able to apply retry logic. That's why we need to cascade the DISABLED state - to make kept-alive workers terminate.
2. I don't think we'd need to "undisable" anything. cascade_disabled_state goes through all tasks, and for each one it might update the state depending on the states of its dependencies. Or are you saying there's some way that luigi itself can "undisable" tasks? I've seen things go from FAILED -> PENDING, but never from DISABLED -> PENDING...

I think we need to stay on this path. The reason I have to pass the config dictionary to luigi.setup_interface_logging() is to ensure that all of the subprocesses that luigi creates will have the correct logging configured. If I were to only call `logging.config.dictConfig(their_dict)` in my "driver" script then the logging config is not correctly set for the worker processes.

To re-clarify: I use a python "driver" script that calls luigi.run() to do my work.  If someone is using the luigi command directly or using python -m luigi, then manually calling `logging.config.dictConfig(their_dict)` might work, but I haven't tried or tested that.

I wouldn't couple it to this PR, but in the future we could probably dramatically speed up the numpy case by using something like [`zipline.utils.numpy_utils.rolling_window`](https://github.com/quantopian/zipline/blob/master/zipline/utils/numpy_utils.py#L253-L319) to implement these as aggregations on a restrided array, which would remove the python overhead of slicing on each iteration.

For example, using `np.mean`, I see about 100x speedup from this:

```python
In [28]: %timeit roll(data, function=np.mean, window=50)
100 loops, best of 3: 3.83 ms per loop

In [29]: %timeit rolling_window(data, 50).mean(axis=1)
10000 loops, best of 3: 35.5 µs per loop
```

For the pandas case, we can just use pandas' native `.rolling()` methods for the single data-input functions.  For the multi-argument functions we'd have to be a little more clever.

> Huh. I would have thought that list.__str__ would have used str on each of item in the list, but I guess that's not the case. Thanks for the improvement.
> 
The reason `str(lst)` uses repr for the elements is that the str representation might have commas, which could make the results very confusing if they did it the way you thought they did:

```
>>> lst = [ "She said,", "'Hello, nice to see you!'", "And I replied, 'Likewise.'" ]
>>> print str(lst)
['She said,', "'Hello, nice to see you!'", "And I replied, 'Likewise.'"]
>>> print repr(lst)
['She said,', "'Hello, nice to see you!'", "And I replied, 'Likewise.'"]
>>> '[ ' + ', '.join([ str(l) for l in lst]) + ' ]'
"[ She said,, 'Hello, nice to see you!', And I replied, 'Likewise.' ]"
```

So `str` and `repr` of a list are equivalent.  (At least in this example -- there might be subtle differences, but I don't think so.)  But the last option is roughly what you expected str to do, and it's pretty hard to parse.

here is a suggestion for what's probably the "right" way to address this:

(1) construct Dataset "from_params"

right now in our config file, we don't really have a notion of a dataset, we just have a `train_data_path` and `test_data_path`, which we pass into `DatasetReader.read()`

probably, though, DatasetReader.read() should take a `Params` object that contains the file path plus other things like "instances_per_epoch" and "max_instances_in_memory" and constructs the Dataset accordingly. 

we could get backward compatibility with config files by checking first for a `train_data` key and then failing back to using `train_data_path` as we do currently. But we would break custom DatasetReaders, unless we called this method something other than `read`

(2) the iterator constructor / from_params takes a Dataset, and then we pass into `train()` the train_iterator and validation_iterator, rather than iterator + train_data + validation_data

this allows us to have different iteration behavior on different datasets, but still only have to specify the iterator params once (the different behaviors are driven by different dataset configs)

This (at least the second part) is sort of the path I was originally going doing with this, before I decided to change the API as little as possible. If we want to do this, I think I'd rather do it in a second PR though.

1. in binary trie, one key can not be the prefix of the other key, e.g, b'\x00\x01' and b'\x00\x01\x02'
2. can not have updates with different values but the same key, otherwise it might result in different root hash after shuffling

so I make some twists:
```
@to_list
def to_kv_pairs(kv_zip):
    for k, v in kv_zip:
        yield (k, v)

@given(k=st.lists(st.binary(min_size=32, max_size=32), min_size=10, max_size=10, unique=True),
    v=st.lists(st.binary(min_size=1), min_size=10, max_size=10),
    set_random=st.random_module())
def test_bin_trie_different_order_insert(k, v, set_random):
    kv_pairs = to_kv_pairs(zip(k, v))
```
Which basically make all keys to be 32 bytes long and values to be 1 bytes minimum.

This is common theme in this file, but as one example, you have `relay_name` registered 7 times, all with identical help and id_part. This creates a maintenance headache for you if you later want to change that stuff.  Instead, you can define an argument type for reuse and use scoping to minimize the amount of rework you might have to do.  This would apply to most arguments here, but I'll use relay_name as an example:

```Python
relay_name_type = CliArgumentType(options_list=['--relay-name'], completer=get_wcfrelay_command_completion_list, id_part='child_name_1', help='Name of WCF Relay.')

with self.argument_context('relay wcfrelay') as c:
  c.argument('relay_name', arg_type=relay_name_type, options_list=['--name', '-n'])

# needed to suppress completer on relay creation
with self.argument_context('relay wcfrelay create') as c:
   c.argument('relay_name', arg_type=relay_name_type, options_list=['--name', '-n'], completer=None)

with self.argument_context('relay wcfrelay authorization-rule') as c:
  c.argument('relay_name', arg_type=relay_name_type)

# needed to suppress `--ids` on child list commands
for scope in ['relay wcfrelay authorization-rule list', 'relay wcfrelay authorization-rule keys list']:
  c.argument('relay_name', arg_type=relay_name_type, id_part=None)
```

By structuring it this way, I eliminate the duplication--help is only defined once--and I reduce the registrations from 7 to 4. If you register metadata for an argument and a particular command doesn't have that argument then the system ignores it, so you can be pretty generic with the scopes at which you register things.

When the connection immediately close, the peer's close notify receive by host after finished the dtls session. so the host interpret this as error, and send back an dtls error message. The peer also has closed the dtls session until now, receive this dtls error, and also send back an dtls error.
Other solutions:
* Bind delayed to socket after close 
* Send no DTLS Error, if no dtls session is open

Currently the `.tranform` method is implemented such that `LabelEncoder.transform(<dask-series>)` returns a dask array. It looks like neither the `assert_eq` in `dask.array.utils` nor `dask.dataframe.utils` can compare a dask array and a dask series (although there could be a utility function I'm unaware of that will do this). 

One way around this could be to remove the `.compute`s and wrap dask series with `da.asarray`. This way `assert_eq` will get two arrays instead of an array and a series. Something like

```python
if isinstance(array, da.Array):
    assert_eq_ar(a.inverse_transform(a.fit_transform(array)), array)
if isinstance(array, dd.Series):
    assert_eq_ar(a.inverse_transform(a.fit_transform(array)), da.asarray(array))
```

Some DNS providers (like Cloudflare) have simple enough APIs that we could use raw requests, but because not all do (see, e.g., ##4448) we eventually need a good story/pattern for packaging and distribution of dependencies. I'd prefer consistently applying that. 

Generally, I also think there are some advantages to using a library/SDK:

- Someone else is responsible for testing that the library actually works with the API. (For the most part, we can focus on testing that we use the library as documented.) 
- Someone else is responsible for testing that the library continues to work when API changes are made. (It's likely that services will test popular libraries/SDKs when making changes.)
- Someone else takes care of things like authentication workflows and translating API errors.

While this case is pretty unlikely, there are a few ways it could happen so it's definitely worth thinking about. Nice catch.

My understanding of what currently happens in this case is we hit this line, see that `$VENV_PATH` exists, delete it, and then rerun the script. In this next run, we hit "phase 1", see that `$OLD_VENV_PATH` exists [here](https://github.com/certbot/certbot/blob/v0.20.0/certbot-auto#L1324), decide not to bootstrap, and run the script again with `--le-auto-phase2` to re-enter "phase 2".

In "phase 2", we check if `$VENV_PATH` exists (which it doesn't), but `$OLD_VENV_PATH` exists, so we delete it, symlink it, and run this script yet again, however, this time, when hitting "phase 1", neither installation location exists so we rebootstrap and everything works.

This is pretty ugly and excessive though. I'll change this up to delete any installation location that exists here, but I'm also going to change this up to check `$OLD_VENV_PATH` more deeply (by specifically looking for `$OLD_VENV_PATH/bin/letsencrypt` as I think one of the cases where both exist is `~/.local/share/letsencrypt` is a location made by the user and not `certbot-auto`.

If you disagree, please let me know!
